Facts.text: Roman Emperor Julian is killed during retreat from the Sassanid Empire. General Jovian is proclaimed Emperor by troops on the battlefield.
St Benedict II begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Battle of Köse Dağ: Mongols under Baiju defeat Seljuk army of Sultan Kaykhusraw II during Mongol attacks in north east Turkey
According to the Lüneburg manuscript, a piper leads 130 children of Hamelin away
Council of Pisa selects Petros Philargi as 3rd Pope: Alexander V
Battle of Chausa: Afghan and northern Indian leader Sher Shah Suri defeats Mughal Emperor Humayun in Bihar in major battle involving 200,000 forces
Maurice, Prince of Orange conquers the Spanish-held Southern Dutch city of Geertruidenberg after a siege of two months, establishing his fame
French expedition led by Pierre Dugua de Monts lands at Île Sainte-Croix to found settlement of Acadia in New France - later moved to Port Royal
Swedish troops under Gustaf II Adolf lands at Peenemunde
Spain & Netherlands sign peace and trade agreement
Peter the Great's son, Tsarevich Alexei Petrovich of Russia dies from the effects of torture after being sentenced to death by his father for plotting against him
Dr Zabdiel Boylston gives 1st smallpox inoculations in America
After a lasting siege and bombardment by cannons, Baku surrenders to the Russians.
Charles Newbold patents 1st cast-iron plow, though farmers fear effects of iron on soil
Lightning hits gunpowder warehouse in Luxembourg; 230 die
Hong Kong proclaimed a British Crown Colony
1st pure food law enacted in US
End of the June Days uprising in Paris by French workers
Battle of Beaver Dam Creek-Union repulses Confederacy in Virginia
Day 2 of 7 Days-Battle of Mechanicsville VA (Meadow Bridge)
US Army of Virginia established under Gen John Pope
1st section of Atlantic City (NJ) Boardwalk opens
Ismael Pasha resigns as khedive of Egypt
Dr Walter Reed begins research that beats Yellow Fever
Japan mobilizes 20,000 troops to help put down the Boxer uprising in China and to advance their long-term interest in gaining land and power in mainland Asia
The Russian Tsar orders that Russian must be the official language of Finland, despite growing unrest within Finland and increasing international concern over Russia's behavior there
Bolsheviks overthrows transport in Tiflis
Victoria & Albert Museum opens in London
Nieuport sets an aircraft speed record of 83 mph (133 kph)
Germany suppresses "Vorwarts" newspaper after it called for peace
Cleveland Indians experiment with numbers on jerseys in a game against Chicago WS; first time MLB players identified by numbers corresponding to those on scorecard
First US troops arrive in France during World War I
The Australian steamer Wimmera is sunk by a mine laid north of Cape Maria van Diemen in 1917 by the German raider Wolf; 26 of its 151 passengers and crew were killed
NY Daily News begins publishing
The emergency decrees under Article 48 of the Weimar Constitution are invoked by the government to deal with deteriorating economic conditions
After 8 years of occupation, US troops leave Dominican Republic
Theatrical revue "Ziegfeld Follies of 1924", featuring Billie Burke and W.C. Fields, opens at the New Amsterdam Theatre, NYC
Comet 7P/Pons-Winnecke approaches within 0.0394 AUs of Earth
The Cyclone roller coaster opens on Coney Island
ENKA/Vereinigte Glanzstoff Factory merge AKU (Genl Kunstzijde Union)
Germany and Poland sign non-aggression treaty

US PresidentFranklin D. Rooseveltsigns National Firearms Act into law, requiring registration and imposition of excise tax on firearms including machine guns. short-barreled shotguns and rifles, and silencers; exceptions made for pistol, revolvers, and long guns
Lloyd Waner sets record of 18 putouts in center in doubleheader
SDAP & CPH achieve majority in city council in Amsterdam
Work service for recent graduates becomes obligatory in Germany
Everett Marshall beats Ali Baba in Columbus, to become wrestling champ
First flight of the Focke-Wulf Fw 61 helicopter, piloted by Ewald Rohlfs
Test cricket debut of all-time great English batsman Len Hutton v New Zealand in 1st Tests at Lord's; scores 0 & 1
Cincinnati Red Lonny Frey hits 8 doubles in a doubleheader
End of USSR experimental calendar; Gregorian readopted 6/27
Lithuanian fascists massacre 2,300 Jews in Kovno
German assault on British railroad terminal at Mersa Matruh, Egypt
2nd British army reaches Grainville-Mouen line
Yanks, Dodgers & Giants play unique 6 inn game for War Bonds, each playing successive innings, final score Dodgers-5, Yanks-1 & Giants-0
England win the second Victory test cricket at Bramall Lane by 41 runs
Dutch Nazi collaborator Arnold Meijer sentenced to 5 years
US begin airlift “Operation Vittles” to West Berlin
1st Belgian parliamentary election where women can vote (CVP gains)
President Gottwald of Czechoslovakia confirms Milada Horakova's death sentence
KCTV (now KLST) TV channel 8 in San Angelo, TX (CBS) 1st broadcast
Jim Petersruns marathon in 2:17:39.4
Freedom Charter signed in South Africa
Mackinac Straits Bridge, Michigan dedicated
Vanguard SLV-2 launched for Earth orbit (failed)
British Somaliland (now Somalia) gains independence from Britain
Hall of Fame allows veteran committee to vote annually
Italian Somaliland declares independence from Italian administration
LPGA Western Open Women's Golf, Beverly CC: Joyce Ziske wins on the second hole of a sudden-death playoff with Barbara Romack
Madagascar (formerly Malagasy Rep) declares independence from France
Blacks begin passive resistance in Cairo Ill
Boston Red Sox Earl Wilson no-hits LA Angels, 2-0
WSEC (now WLRN) TV channel 17 in Miami, FL (PBS) begins broadcasting
Alfons Gorbach forms Austrian government
Dutch Second Chamber condemns commercial TV
Moise Tsjombe forms government in Congo
Wallon party forms in Belgium
Kanton Bazel leads female suffrage in Switzerland
10-year-old English girl Mary Bell strangles four-year-old Martin Brown in Newcastle-upon-Tyne (later convicted of manslaughter on grounds of diminished responsibility)
Executive Council decides both AL & NL to divide into 2 divisions
Frank Robinsonhits 2 grand slams as Baltimore Orioles beat Washington Senators 12-2
Two young girls die in a premature explosion in Derry after their father, a member of the Irish Republican Army, was making an incendiary device, presumably for use against the British Army
Angels suspendAlex Johnson(after 5 benchings & 29 fines)
Australian swing bowler Bob Massie takes 16 wickets (8-84 & 8-53) on Test cricket debut in 2nd Test vs England at Lord's
The Provisional Irish Republican Army (IRA) kill two British Army soldiers in separate attacks during the day and at midnight begins a "bi-lateral truce"
On Plesetsk Cosmodrome, USSR, 9 people are killed in an explosion of a Cosmos 3-M rocket.
The Universal Product Code (UPC) is scanned for the first time to sell a package of Wrigley's chewing gum at Marsh Supermarket in Troy, Ohio

Two FBI agents and a member of the American Indian Movement are killed in a shootout on the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation in South Dakota; Leonard Peltier is later convicted of the murders in a controversial trial.

U.S. Supreme Court's rules unanimously in "O'Connor v. Donaldson" that non-dangerous people can't be confined to psychiatric facilities without adequate treatment if able to live viably in outside society
CN Tower opens in Toronto - at 553 metres (1,815 feet), the tallest freestanding structure in the world (surpassed by Dubai’s Burj Khalifa 2009)
Washington Senators player Toby Harrah plays an entire doubleheader at shortstop without a single chance in the field
42 die in a fire caused by an inmate at Maury County Jail in Columbia, Tennessee
The Yorkshire Ripper kills 16-year-old shop assistant Jayne MacDonald in Leeds, changing public perception of the killer as she was the first victim who was not a prostitute.
Tim Severin aboard the Brendan reaches Newfoundland in attempt to prove sixth-century Irish monks could have voyaged across the Atlantic
Air Canada Flight 189 to Toronto overruns the runway and crashes into the Etobicoke Creek ravine, killing two of the 107 passengers on board
Brittany separatists bomb Palace of Versailles in France
First dedicated oceanographic satellite, SEASAT 1, launched
British rock drummer Nigel Olsson runs a stop sign, accidentally crashes, and kills another driver
The Village People become the first disco act to play Madison Square Garden, NYC
Carlos Lopes runs European record 10km (27:34.39)
US vetos UN Security Council resolution for a limited withdrawal from Beirut of Israeli & Palestine Liberation Organization forces
"Loving" premieres on TV
1st flight of Shuttle Discovery (41-D) scrubbed at T -4
Losing 9-0 to Red Sox, Yanks score 11 in 3rd & win 12-11 in 10 inn
Supreme Court rules 16-year-olds can receive the death penalty
122°F in Phoenix, Arizona
African National Congress (ANC) leaderNelson Mandelaaddresses a joint session of US Congress in Washington, D.C.
Eight Kansas and Oklahoma radio stations boycott k d lang, due to her anti-meat ad
New Kid on the Block Donnie Wahlberg release from hospital after fall
Amy Elizabeth Goodman, of California, crowned America's Junior Miss
NBA Draft: UNLV power forward Larry Johnson first pick by Charlotte Hornets
India leases Tin Bigha corridor to Bangladesh
NYC's MTA votes to ban cigarette ads on Jan 1, 1993
Supreme Court rules fund soliciting can be banned at airports
NHL Draft: Victoriaville Tigres (QMJHL) center Alexandre Daigle first pick by Ottawa Senators
NY MetEddie Murrayis 20th to get 1,600 RBIs
Rebecca Jones, 18, of Georgia, crowned America's Junior Miss
104°F (40°C) at Denver, Colorado
107°F (41.6°C) at Albuquerque, New Mexico
112°F (44.4°C) at El Paso, Texas
122°F (50°C) at Laughlin, Nevada
126°F (52.2°C) in Death Valley, California
Air Ivory Fokker-27 crashes at Abidjan (16 killed/1 lives)
Kirby Puckettpass Rod Carew with 2,088 hit as Twin's top hit leader
Hamad bin Khalifa al-Thani deposes his father Khalifa bin Hamad al-Thani, the Emir of Qatar, in a bloodless coup.

Karlheinz Stockhausen's musical piece "Helikopter-Streichquartett" premieres in and above Amsterdam - members of a string quartet perform in individual helicopters flying independent flight paths over Holland with sounds and video transmitted and mixed to  audience in the concert hall
Irish Journalist Veronica Guerin is shot in her car while in traffic in the outskirts of Dublin
NBA Draft: Georgetown guard Allen Iverson first pick by Philadelphia 76ers
Galileo, Ganymede Observations (Orbit 9)
The U.S. Supreme Court upholds doctor-assisted suicide ban
U.S. Supreme Court strikes down the Communications Decency Act, inconsistent with the 1st Amendment
NHL Draft: Long Beach Ice Dogs (IHL) center Patrik Stefan first pick by Atlanta Thrashers
NBA Draft: Shanghai Sharks (China) center Yao Ming first pick by Houston Rockets
The U.S. Supreme Court rules in Lawrence v. Texas that gender-based sodomy laws are unconstitutional.
NBA Draft: Memphis point guard Derrick Rose first pick by Chicago Bulls
The U.S. Supreme Court rules in District of Columbia v. Heller that the ban on handguns in the District of Columbia is unconstitutional.
Police procedural drama "Line of Duty" debuts on BBC 2 in the UK
20 people are killed after a rescue helicopter crashes in Uttarakhand
Kevin Rudd defeats Julia Gillard in a leadership battle to become Australian Prime Minister
Following the military coup in Thailand, people are warned that anyone calling for protest on social media will be prosecuted for sedition
Joel Embiidis selected with the third overall pick by the Philadelphia 76ers in the 2014 NBA draft
NBA Draft: Kansas small forward Andrew Wiggins first pick by Cleveland Cavaliers
Gunman opens fire at beach resort in Sousse, Tunisia, killing 38. Isis claims responsibility.
NHL Draft: Erie Otters (OHL) center Connor McDavid #1 pick by the Edmonton Oilers
Saudi suicide bomber kills 27 at Shia Imam al-Sadiq mosque in Kuwait, 227 injured
US Supreme Court rules 5-4 same-sex marriage is a legal right across all US states
City of Falluja freed from Islamic State control after a month-long campaign by Iraqi forces
Iceland’s presidential election is won by history professor Guðni Jóhannesson
Panama Canal's third set of locks opens for commercial traffic, doubling the Canal’s capacity at an estimated cost of $5.25 billion
Helicopter with rogue police pilot attacks Venezuelan Supreme Court in Caracas
First high level talks between Ethiopian and Eritrean officials in 20 years begins in Addis Ababa
Duke of Gloucester succeeds as KingRichard III of Englandafter Parliament declaredEdward Villegitimate
Christ's Hospital in London, England, granted a charter by KingEdward VIto house and teach children of the poor
Ellen G. Whiteis baptized by John Hobart in Portland, Maine
The first 62 recipients receive the Victoria Cross for valor in the Crimean War fromQueen Victoria
Richard Wagner's opera "Die Walküre" (The Valkyrie), the second in his Ring Cycle, premieres in Munich, featuring "Ride of the Valkyries"
Karl Benzof Germany receives US patent for gasoline-driven auto
Aga Khan IIIis appointed Knight Grand Commander of the Order of the Indian Empire
Gustav Mahler's 9th Symphony premieres in Vienna, Austria with Bruno Walter conducting the Vienna Philharmonic

The Indian Relief Act, passes after a protracted period of Passive Resistance led byGandhi; it abolishes a £3 tax imposed on Indians who had not renewed their indentures and recognizes "the validity of Indian customary marriages"
Silent film "The Gold Rush," directed, starring, and written byCharlie Chaplin, is released and becomes the fifth highest-grossing silent film of all time
First meeting of the International Congresses of Modern Architects (CIAM) at Lake Leman, Switzerland organized byLe Corbusier
US PresidentFranklin D. Rooseveltsigns Federal Credit Union Act into law, establishing Credit Unions
W.E.B. Du Boisresigns position at NAACP
Fred Allen's last radio show, his guest isJack Benny
Nelson Mandelaand 51 others infringe South Africa's curfew
Lavrentiy Beria, one of the trio of Soviet leaders after Stalin's death and the former secret police chief, is ousted from power and arrested
Leading a minority government,Gaston Eyskensis selected Prime Minister of Belgium for the second time
Ingemar Johanssonof Sweden floorsFloyd Patterson7 times in the 3rd round in NYC to win the world heavyweight boxing title
Queen Elizabeth IIand US PresidentDwight D. Eisenhoweropen the St. Lawrence Seaway, allowing oceangoing vessels to travel from the Atlantic Ocean to the Great Lakes of North America
Levi Eshkol replacesDavid Ben-Gurionas Israeli Prime Minister
PopePaul VInames 27 new cardinals including Karol Józef Wojtyła, the Archbishop of Cracow and future PopeJohn Paul II
London production of  Jim Jacobs and Warren Casey's  musical "Grease", starringRichard Gere, opens at the New London Theatre
Indian Prime MinisterIndira Gandhideclares a state of emergency
Japanese professional wrestler Antonio Inoki fights American boxerMuhammad Ali, at the Nippon Budokan in Tokyo, Japan
Elvis Presleyappears in concert at Market Square Arena in Indianapolis, Indiana; unknowingly the last performance of his career
"Moonraker", 11th James Bond film starringRoger Moore, premieres in London
"Stripes", American military comedy film, directed and produced byIvan Reitman, starringBill Murray, Harold Ramis, andJohn Candypremieres
Portugal's Socialist Prime MinisterMário Soaresresigns amid predictions that Parliament would be dissolved and elections called
Columbia Records releases "King's Record Shop", the sixth studio album by American singer-songwriterRosanne Cash, and the final of her albums produced by husband Rodney Crowell
Supreme Court JusticeLewis F. Powell Jr.announces his retirement
Canada updates its coinage with a new portrait ofQueen Elizabeth II, designed by a Canadian for the 1st time - artist Dora dePedery-Hunt
US launches a cruise missile attack targeting Baghdad's intelligence headquarters in retaliation for a thwarted assassination attempt against former PresidentGeorge H. W. Bushin April in Kuwait
PLO leaderYasser Arafatreturns to Gaza after 27 years
Gunmen ambush Egyptian PresidentHosni Mubarakwho escapes unharmed
Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone, 1st book inJ. K. Rowling's best-selling series, is published
NBA Draft: St. Vincent–St. Mary HS (Akron, Ohio) small forwardLeBron Jamesfirst pick by Cleveland Cavaliers
"Good Time" by Owl City (Adam Young) andCarly Rae Jepsenreleases, topping charts worldwide and selling 2.24 million copies in the US by the end of 2012
Animated film "Ice Age: Continental Drift", directed by Steve Martino and Mike Thurmeier with voices byRay RomanoandJohn Leguizamopremieres in Buenos Aires
Peter Ustinov's "The Moment of Truth" opens at Southwark Playhouse in London starringBonnie Wright(Ginny Weasley)
Luis Suárezis expelled from the 2014 FIFA World Cup following his biting incident
Greek Prime MinisterAlexis Tsiprascalls for a referendum on new bailout terms during the Greek Credit Crisis
US PresidentBarack Obamasings "Amazing Grace" as part of his eulogy for Reverend Clementa Pinckney and 8 church members killed during a bible study in Charleston, South Carolina
America's Cup: EmiratesTeam New Zealanddefeats Oracle Team USA 7-1 in Bermuda, makingPeter Burling(26) the youngest helmsman to win the cup
Brazilian state prosecutors file corruption charges against PresidentMichel Temer
Theresa May's UK Conservative minority party strikes a deal to govern with Northern Ireland's DUP, guaranteeing £1 billion in funding for Northern Ireland
"TheBee Gees" singer-songwriterBarry Gibbis knighted byPrince Charlesat Buckingham Palace

Battle of Fleurus: Major victory by forces of the First French Republic under General Jean-Baptiste Jourdan over the Coalition Army (Great Britain, Hanover, Dutch Republic, and Habsburgs) with the first use of a reconnaissance balloon
The United Nations Charter is signed by 50 nations in San Francisco
US PresidentJohn F. Kennedygives his famous "Ich bin ein Berliner" (intended to mean "I am a Berliner", but may actually mean "I am a doughnut") speech in West Berlin
Iwo Jima & Bonin Islands returned to Japan by US
Anne Catherine of Brandenburg, Queen of Denmark and Norway, born in Halle (Saale), Holy Roman Empire (now Germany) (d. 1612)
Johannes Schultz, German composer, born in Lüneburg, Hanseatic League (d. 1653)
Hedvig Sophia, Duchess of Holstein-Gottorp, born in Three Crowns Castle, Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1708)
Edward Holyoke, American academic and 9th President of Harvard University, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1769)
Georg Brandt, Swedish chemist and mineralogist, born in Riddarhyttan, Sweden (d. 1768)
Philip Doddridge, English Nonconformist clergyman, born in London (d. 1751)
Thomas Clap, American academic, 1st President of Yale University, born in Scituate, Massachusetts (d. 1767)
Arthur Middleton, American signer (Declaration of Independence), born in Charleston, South Carolina (d. 1787)
Leopold Koželuch [Jan Antonín Koželuh], Czech pianist, composer, kapellmeister, and teacher, born in Velvary, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1818)[1]
Louis Joseph Claude Saint-Amans, French composer, born in Marseille, France (d. 1820)
Johan I Jozef, Monarch of Liechtenstein and fieldmarshal, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1836)
George Morland, English artist of rural landscapes, born in London (d. 1804)
Branwell Brontë [Patrick Branwell Brontë], English painter and writer and brother of the writers Charlotte, Emily and Anne, born in Thornton, West Yorkshire, England (d. 1848)
Abner Doubleday, American Union Army General-Major, and inventor (San Francisco cable cars), born in Ballston Spa, New York (d. 1893)
Bartolomé Mitre [Martínez], Argentine general, author, statesman and President (1862-1868), born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1906)

Victor Girardey, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Lauw, France (d. 1864)
Bernard Berenson, American art critic (Italian Painters of the Renaissance), born in Vilnius, Lithuania, Russian Empire (d. 1959)
Charles Bonin, French explorer and diplomat (China), born in Poissy, France (d. 1929)
George C. Pearce, American actor (The Shadow Sinister, British Agent, Valiant), born in New York City (d. 1940)

George Herbert, 5th Earl of Carnarvon, English financier and Egyptologist who funded the search for and excavation of Tutankhamun's tomb in the Valley of the Kings, born in Highclere Castle, Hampshire, England (d. 1923)
Josef Swickard, German actor (The Wizard of Oz; Lost City; A Tale of Two Cities), born in Coblenz, Germany (d. 1940)
Martin Andersen Nexø, Danish writer (Pelle Erobreren), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1954)
Albert Relf, English cricket all-rounder (13 Tests, 1 x 50, 25 wickets, BB 5/85; Sussex CCC, Auckland CA), born in Burwash, East Sussex, England (d. 1937)
Camille Zeckwer, American pianist, organist, composer, and educator (Philadelphia Musical Academy, 1917-24), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1924)
Albert Siklós, Hungarian composer, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1942)
Ernest Torrence, Scottish actor (I Cover the Waterfront, Hunchback of Notre Dame), born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1933)
Natalia Brassova, Russian noblewoman, wife of Grand Duke Michael Alexandrovich of Russia, born in Perovo, Moscow, Russian Empire (d. 1952)
Ya'akov Cahan, Hebrew poet and writer, born in Slutsk, Belarus (d. 1960)
Anna Maria Franciska van Wageningen-Salomons, Dutch author (Girl Student), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1980)
Anthony Gustav de Rothschild, British philanthropist, born in London (d. 1961)
Paul Niggli, Swiss mineralogist (crystal structures), born in Zonfingen, Switzerland (d. 1953)
Jeanne Eagels, American actress and former Ziegfeld Girl (Rain, Under False Colors), born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1929)
Heinrich Lemacher, German composer, born in Solingen, Dusseldorf, Germany (d. 1966)
Sidney Coe Howard, American dramatist (Swords, Pulitzer 1925), born in Oakland, California (d. 1939)
"Big" Bill Broonzy, American blues singer and guitarist (Blues by Broonzy), born in Scott, Mississippi (d. 1958)

Dorothy Fuldheim, American print and broadcast journalist (The Cleveland Press; WEWS-TV, Cleveland, Ohio), credited as being 1st woman in US to anchor a television news broadcast, born in Passiac, New Jersey (d. 1989)
Bill Wirges, American orchestra leader (Growing Paynes), born in Buffalo, New York (d. 1971)
Jankel Adler, Polish painter and printmaker, born in Tuszyn, Łódź, Poland (d. 1949)
Viola Dana, American actress (Willow Tree, 40 Winks, Silent Lover), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1987)
Chesty Puller, US Marine Corps lieutenant general and the most decorated Marine in history, born in West Point, Virginia (d. 1971)
Willy Messerschmitt, German aircraft designer, born in Frankfurt am Main, Germany (d. 1978)
Maria Nikolaevna, 3rd daughter of the last Russian Tsar, Nicholas II and Tsarina Alexandra Feodorovna, born in Peterhof Palace, Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1918)
Jo Spier [Joseph Spier], Dutch cartoonist and water color painter, born in Zutphen, Netherlands (d. 1978)
Stuart Symington, U.S. senator from Missouri (1953–76), born in Amherst, Massachusetts (d. 1988)
William Busch, British composer, born in London (d. 1945)
Antonia Brico, Dutch-American conductor and pianist, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1989)
Bill Lear, American engineer, manufacturer and CEO (Lear Jet Corp), born in Hannibal, Missouri (d. 1978)
Hugues Cuénod, Swiss tenor and musical educator, born in Corseaux-sur-Vevey, Switzerland (d. 2010)
"St. Louis" Jimmy Oden, American blues singer-songwriter ("Goin' Down Slow", "Take the Bitter with the Sweet"), born in Nashville, Tennessee (d. 1977)
Ashley Clarke, British diplomat to Italy and Chairman of the Venice in Peril Fund, born in Stourbridge, England (d. 1994)
Babe Herman, American baseball right fielder (.324 career average; hit for cycle record x 3; Brooklyn Robins), born in Buffalo, New York (d. 1987)
Frank Scott Hogg, Canadian astronomer, born in Preston, Ontario (d. 1951)
Peter Lorre, Hungarian-American actor (M, Casablanca, Beast with 5 Fingers), born in Rózsahegy, Austria-Hungary (d. 1964)
Jack Longland, British director of education (Derbyshire), born in London (d. 1993)
Jan Louis Guillaume Doornik, Dutch resistance fighter (Free French Forces), born in Paris (d. 1941)
Teddy Grace [Stella Crowson], American jazz and blues singer, born in Arcadia, Louisiana (d. 1992)[1]
Stefan Andres, German writer (Wir sind Utopia), born in Dhrönchen (d. 1970)
Viktor Schreckengost, American industrial designer, born in Sebring, Ohio (d. 2008)

Jimmy McLin, American jazz banjo player, and guitarist (James P. Johnson; Willie "The Lion" Smith; The Ink Spots), born in Brooksville, Florida (d. 1983)
Betty Askwith, British writer and biographer, know for "A Tangled Web", born in London, England (d. 1995)
Colonel Tom Parker [Andreas Cornelis van Kuijk], Dutch-born talent manager (Elvis Presley), born in Breda, Netherlands (d. 1997)
Nicholas Polunin, British botanist and environmentalist, born in Checkendon, Oxfordshire (d. 1997)
Charles Spry, Australian soldier and security chief, born in Brisbane, Queensland (d. 1994)
Roy J. Plunkett, American chemist and inventor of Teflon, born in New Carlisle, Ohio (d. 1994)[1]

Aimé Césaire, French Martinican poet and politician, born in Basse-Pointe, Martinique (d. 2008)
Maurice Wilkes, British computer scientist and inventor (stored program concept for computers), born in Dudley, England (d. 2010)
Laurie Lee, English poet and author (I Can't Stay Long), born in Slad, Gloucestershire, England (d. 1997)
Richard Maltby Sr., American musician and orchestra leader (Vaughn Monroe Show), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1991)
Shapour Bakhtiar, Iranian politician (Prime Minister, for 36 days in 1979), born in Shahrekord, Persia (d. 1991)
Wolfgang Windgassen, German operatic tenor (Stuttgart Opera), born in Annemasse, France (d. 1974)
Charlotte Zolotow, American author and publisher of children's books (When the Wind Stops), born in Norfolk, Virginia (d. 2013)
Paul Castellano, American organized-crime chief, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1985)

Wille Hamilton, British MP, born in Houghton-le-Spring, England (d. 2000)
Roger Voisin, American classical trumpet player (Boston Symphony, 1935-83), educator, and music editor, born in Angers, France (d. 2008)
Peter Rawlinson, Baron Rawlinson of Ewell, British MP and attorney general, born in Birkenhead, England (d. 2006)
June Preisser, American acrobatic dancer and actress (Babes in Arms, Strike Up the Band), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1984)
Leonid Hambro, American concert pianist and composer, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2006)
Abe Jan Koldijk, Dutch physician for the Nazi regime (Limburg's family doctor), born in Vreeland, Netherlands (d. 2015)
Violette Szabo, French WWII secret agent, born in Paris, France (d. 1945)
Alan T. Peacock, British economist, born in Ryton, England (d. 2014)
Campbell Adamson, British industrialist and CEO (Abbey National Pictures), born in Perth, Scotland (d. 2000)
Eleanor Parker, American actress (Caged, Detective Story, Hans Brinker), born in Cedarville, Ohio (d. 2013)
Barbara Graham, American criminal convicted of murder, born in Oakland, California (d. 1955)
David Haslam, British rear admiral (hydrographer), born in Derby, England (d. 2009)
Kostas Axelos, Greek-French philosopher, born in Athens, Greece (d. 2010)
Peter Miles, British Army officer, businessman and courtier in the Household of Elizabeth II, born in Long Ashton, Somerset (d. 2013)
Syd Lawrence, British bandleader (Syd Lawrence Orchestra), born in Wilmslow, Cheshire, England (d. 1998)
Pavel Belyayev, Soviet fighter pilot and cosmonaut (Voskhod II), born in Chelishchevo, Russia (d. 1970)
Tadeusz Konwicki, Polish writer (Bohin Manor) and film director, born in Nowa Wilejka, Wilenskie (d. 2015)

Yoshiro Nakamatsu, Japanese inventor, born in Tokyo, Japan
Alexander Fenton, CEO (Euro Ethnological Research Center in Edinburgh), born in Shotts, Lanarkshire (d. 2012)
Francesco Cossiga, Italian politician and 8th President of the Italian Republic (1985-92), born in Sassari, Kingdom of Italy (d. 2010)
Ian Prestt, English ornithologist, born in Lancaster (d. 1995)
Milton Glaser, American graphic designer (founder of New York Magazine; creator of "I ♥ NY" logo), born in New York City (d. 2020)
Patrick Ryan, Irish priest who funneled funds and weapons for the I.R.A, born in Rossmore, Ireland (d. 2025)[1]
Peter Feuchtwanger, German piano teacher, musicologist, and composer (Studies in Eastern Idiom; Introduction and Ragas for 8 Violas and cello), born in Munich, Germany (d. 2016)
Colin Wilson, English author (Afterlife, Book of Great Mysteries), born in Leicester, Leicestershire, England (d. 2013)
Lucien Goethals, Belgian composer (Polyfonium, Pluriversum), born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 2006)
Harry Bromfield, South African cricket spin bowler (9 Tests, 17 wickets; Western Province), born in Mossel Bay, South Africa (d. 2020)
Claudio Abbado, Italian conductor (La Scala, 1969-86; London Symphony, 1979-87; Berlin Philharmonic, 1989-2002), born in Milan, Italy (d. 2014)
David Winnick, British Labour Party MP, born in Brighton, Sussex, England
